source_note: Code entries are sourced exclusively from text extracted directly from the lab report PDFs. Appendix code blocks in Labs 3A, 3B, 4, 5A, and 5B were embedded as image screenshots and are therefore not extractable as text. All inline code snippets that appeared as readable text in the report body are preserved verbatim.

labs.title: Lab 1A: Open-Loop Simulation
objectives: Introduce system modeling using Simulink
Analyze first-order and second-order dynamic systems
Observe time responses of mass-damper, first-order, and second-order systems to step inputs
Understand how system parameters (gain, damping ratio, time constants) affect system behavior
prelab_questions: How does the time constant affect the speed of a first-order system response?
What is the role of the damping ratio in a second-order system?
How do you calculate steady-state gain from a step response?
What is the difference between overdamped, underdamped, and critically damped responses?
Does the system behave as expected? How long does it take to reach 63.2% of its final value?
What is the size of the output step?
How could you change the system to produce the same output but twice as fast?
How could you double the output?
What effect does doubling the damping coefficient have?
What effect does doubling the oscillation frequency have?
Do two first-order systems in series constitute a second-order system?
What role does each subsystem play in determining the response?
important_equations: First-order TF: G(s) = K / (tau*s + 1)
Second-order TF: G(s) = K*wn^2 / (s^2 + 2*zeta*wn*s + wn^2)
Mass-damper ODE: m*dv/dt + b*v = F
Time constant tau: time to reach 63.2% of final value
Steady-state gain K = steady_state_output / step_input_magnitude
Settling time ~4*tau for first-order systems
Series two first-order TFs: G_total(s) = G1(s)*G2(s)
matlab_simulink_exercises: Exercise 1: Model mass-damper system using differential equation blocks in Simulink
Exercise 2: Implement first-order systems using Transfer Function blocks; vary K and tau
Exercise 3: Model second-order systems with standard TF; vary zeta and wn
Exercise 4: Connect two first-order systems in series; plot combined response in MATLAB
Adjust simulation stop time and solver tolerance for accuracy

matlab_code_verbatim.source: Appendix, Figure 15 — Example MATLAB Plotting Code Snippet

common_mistakes: Confusing time constant with settling time (settling ~4*tau, not tau)
Forgetting that two first-order systems in series form a second-order system (but without oscillation)
Assuming increased gain always improves performance without checking stability
Misreading 63.2% threshold for time constant from plot
Not adjusting solver tolerance, leading to inaccurate simulation results
likely_exam_concepts: Identify system order from step response shape
Extract time constant and steady-state gain from a plot
Predict effect of doubling gain or halving time constant
Sketch expected step response for given zeta and wn values
Build/interpret a Simulink block diagram for first/second-order TF
Effect of damping ratio on overshoot and oscillation
Effect of natural frequency on response speed
What does a step response of two series first-order systems look like?

labs.title: Lab 1B: Closed-Loop Simulation
objectives: Introduce closed-loop control systems
Analyze behavior of proportional, integral, and derivative (PID) controllers
Observe how controller actions influence system response, steady-state error, and stability
Explore effects of controller gain tuning and actuator saturation on system performance
prelab_questions: What does each term (P, I, D) contribute to controller behavior?
Why does proportional control alone leave steady-state error?
What is actuator saturation and why does it matter?
How does increasing Kp affect rise time and overshoot?
What is steady-state error and how is it calculated?
important_equations: PID control law: u(t) = Kp*e(t) + Ki*integral(e) + Kd*de/dt
Steady-state error (proportional only): e_ss = 1 / (1 + Kp*K_plant)
Closed-loop TF: G_cl(s) = G_ol(s) / (1 + G_ol(s))
For tank system: G(s) = K / (tau*s + 1), steady-state error = 1/(1+Kp*K)
Percent steady-state error = |ref - output_ss| / ref * 100%
Damping ratio from closed-loop poles: zeta = real(pole) / |pole|
matlab_simulink_exercises: Exercise 5: Model PID controller independently; observe individual P, I, D actions to step input
Exercise 6: Control first-order tank system with proportional feedback; vary Kp
Part (a): Kp baseline — observe steady-state error (~0.0909 inches for 1-inch reference)
Part (b): Step increase and decrease — test bidirectional tracking
Part (c): Increase Kp until steady-state error < 10%
Part (d): Design for fast response — steady-state error 6.25%, time constant ~1.3 s
Part (e): Add Saturation block; observe nonlinear behavior when valve command hits limits
Part (f): Redesign controller to eliminate saturation while maintaining acceptable performance
Exercise 7: Second-order closed-loop system; compare Kp=1 vs Kp=2 step responses

matlab_code_verbatim.source: Appendix, Figure 19 — Example MATLAB Script: Plotting, Calculations, Annotations
matlab_code_verbatim.description: Full inline script from report body. Computes y_ss, percent_error, and tau from Simulink output, then annotates the plot.

controller_tuning_methods: Increase Kp to reduce steady-state error and speed up response
Balance Kp against saturation limits — high Kp causes actuator saturation
Observe that P-only control cannot eliminate steady-state error for type-0 plant
Add integral term to eliminate steady-state error
Tradeoff: faster response (higher Kp) -> more oscillation, less damping
common_mistakes: Thinking proportional control eliminates steady-state error (it does NOT for type-0 plants)
Ignoring actuator saturation when designing high-gain controllers
Confusing open-loop gain with closed-loop gain
Not recognizing that derivative action produces a sharp spike at a step input
Forgetting integral action causes a continuously increasing output for a constant error
Setting Kp too high causes system to go unstable or saturate
likely_exam_concepts: Describe individual P, I, D actions to a step input
Calculate steady-state error for a given Kp and plant gain
Build Simulink closed-loop block diagram with PID controller
Explain effect of saturation on closed-loop performance
Determine Kp needed to achieve steady-state error < X%
Predict how doubling Kp changes damping ratio and overshoot
Identify underdamped vs overdamped closed-loop response from poles
Why does integral action eliminate steady-state error?

labs.title: Lab 2: Python Simulation — Rocket Booster Pendulum (P and PD Control)
objectives: Simulate and control the vertical landing of a rocket booster modeled as a pendulum
Implement proportional (P) and proportional-derivative (PD) controllers in Python
Analyze effects of controller gains on system response (overshoot and settling time)
Tune controller gains to meet design requirements: <5% overshoot, settling time <5 seconds
prelab_questions: What is the control law for a proportional controller?
What is the control law for a PD controller?
How does Kp affect settling time?
How does Kd affect percent overshoot?
Write a Python function that accepts one input n and outputs the first n-th Fibonacci number and their sum
important_equations: P control law: u = Kp * e
PD control law: u = Kp * e + Kd * edot  (where edot is the derivative of the error)
Error: e = desired_angle - measured_angle
Percent overshoot %OS = (peak - final) / final * 100%
Settling time: time to stay within 2% band of final value

labs.title: Lab 3A: Step Response Test (DC Motor Characterization)
objectives: Characterize the dynamic response of a DC motor using a step input
Determine steady-state gain and time constant from experimental velocity response
Develop a first-order transfer function model of the motor
Validate the model by comparing simulated vs experimental step response
prelab_questions: What is a first-order system and what are its key parameters?
How do you extract the time constant from a step response plot?
How do you calculate steady-state gain from a step response?
What is the utime module in MicroPython used for?
What is the encoder used for in this experiment?
important_equations: Steady-state gain: K = omega_ss / V_input  (925 rad/s / 24 V = 38.5 rad/s/V)
Time constant tau: time to reach 63.2% of steady-state velocity (0.23 s)
First-order TF: G(s) = K / (tau*s + 1)
Sampling interval: 1 / 500 Hz = 2 ms
Settling time ~4*tau (time within 2% of steady state)
labs.appendix_code_note: main.py (Appendix A) was submitted as an image screenshot and is not extractable as text. The report body text describes the file's purpose and explicitly names the utime functions used.
reported_api_calls_verbatim: utime.ticks_us() — used to track the current time in microseconds
utime.ticks_diff() — used to compute the elapsed time between samples
motor.py — provided MicroPython module, uploaded to microcontroller using Thonny
encoder.py — provided MicroPython module, uploaded to microcontroller using Thonny
main.py — developed to perform a step response test
labs.reported_sampling_logic_verbatim: Data was recorded only when the elapsed time exceeded the predefined sampling interval, ensuring a consistent sampling rate throughout the experiment.
labs.reported_data_flow_verbatim: The measured velocity data was stored in queues and later transmitted to a PC via serial communication.

python_coding_exercises.source: Appendix A (image — not extractable as text)
python_coding_exercises.described_behavior: Drives motor at constant 100% voltage (24V step), samples encoder at 500 Hz using utime, stores data in queues, transmits to PC via serial.
python_coding_exercises.sampling_condition_verbatim: Data was recorded only when the elapsed time exceeded the predefined sampling interval

code_completion_exercises.title: MicroPython timed sampling pattern
code_completion_exercises.instructions: Fill in the utime calls described in the Lab 3A report to implement consistent 500 Hz sampling.
code_completion_exercises.blanked_code:
import utime
from motor import Motor
from encoder import Encoder

motor = Motor()
encoder = Encoder()

SAMPLING_INTERVAL_US = 2000   # 500 Hz -> 2 ms -> 2000 us

motor.set_effort(100)         # 100% = 24 V step input

t_last = _____________         # (1) record start time in microseconds

while collecting:
    t_now = _____________     # (2) get current time in microseconds
    elapsed = _____________   # (3) compute elapsed time using ticks_diff

    if elapsed >= SAMPLING_INTERVAL_US:
        vel = encoder.get_velocity()
        data_queue.append(vel)
        t_last = t_now
code_completion_exercises.note: The report explicitly names utime.ticks_us() and utime.ticks_diff() as the two functions used. The sampling condition 'data was recorded only when elapsed time exceeded the predefined sampling interval' maps directly to the if-statement shown.

hardware_procedures: Connect STM32 microcontroller to DC motor and rotary encoder
Upload motor.py and encoder.py to STM32 using Thonny
Run main.py: apply constant 100% voltage (24V) step input to motor
Sample angular velocity at 500 Hz using utime module
Store data in queues, transmit to PC via serial communication
Plot velocity vs time in Jupyter Notebook; identify K=38.5 and tau=0.23 s
common_mistakes: Reading time constant at 63.2% but measuring from wrong baseline
Using percentage (100%) instead of actual voltage (24V) when calculating steady-state gain
Subtracting ticks directly instead of using utime.ticks_diff() — ticks can wrap
Confusing settling time (1.0 s) with time constant (0.23 s)
Not accounting for motor driver gain (24V / 100% = 0.24 V/%) when building the Simulink model
likely_exam_concepts: Given a step response plot, calculate K and tau
Write MicroPython timed sampling using utime.ticks_us() and utime.ticks_diff()
Build and validate a Simulink first-order model from identified parameters
Explain why utime.ticks_diff() must be used instead of direct subtraction
Explain why experimental and simulated step responses differ

labs.title: Lab 3B: Closed-Loop Velocity Control (PI Controller on Microcontroller)
objectives: Implement a PI controller using object-oriented programming in MicroPython
Deploy controller on STM32 microcontroller for closed-loop velocity control
Test and evaluate P-only, I-only, and PI controller performance using step-response tests
Compare experimental results with theoretical Simulink model
Perform hand calculations for theoretical time constant and steady-state values
prelab_questions: What does proportional control do to steady-state error vs response speed?
What does integral control do to steady-state error vs transient response?
How do you identify controller saturation graphically?
What are closed-loop poles and what do they tell you about stability?
Write an OOP Python class with __init__, set_setpoint, set_gains, and run methods
important_equations: PI control law: u(t) = Kp*e(t) + Ki*integral(e(t)dt)
Motor model: G_motor(s) = Kss / (tau*s + 1)  [Kss=46, omega=4 -> tau=1/4=0.25]
Motor driver gain: 24/100 = 0.24
Motor model including driver: G(s) = (0.24 * Kss) / (tau*s + 1)
PI controller TF: C(s) = Kp + Ki/s = (Kp*s + Ki) / s
Open-loop TF: L(s) = C(s) * G(s)
Closed-loop TF: T(s) = L(s) / (1 + L(s))
Closed-loop poles: roots of denominator of T(s)

identified_gains_verbatim.source: Report body text, Discussion section: 'Using: Kp = 0.19  Ki = 0.5  Kss = 46  omega = 4  Motor driver gain = 24/100 = 0.24'

controller_tuning_methods: P-only: fast response but steady-state error remains
I-only: eliminates steady-state error but very slow transient
PI combined: fast response + steady-state error eliminated
Both closed-loop poles real and negative -> stable and overdamped
Saturation identified graphically when controller output flattens at +/-100% limit
common_mistakes: Not resetting the integral accumulator between test runs
Forgetting to include motor driver gain (0.24) in the Simulink model
Large Ki: integral continues accumulating past setpoint, causing overshoot
Not checking for saturation — control effort exceeds +/-100%
Using class name 'Controller' instead of 'controller' (report uses lowercase)
likely_exam_concepts: Write the controller.py OOP class with the exact method names from the report
Calculate open-loop and closed-loop transfer functions for PI + motor
Find closed-loop poles and determine stability and damping type
Explain why P-only has steady-state error and I-only is slow
Identify controller saturation on a plot
Build Simulink model for PI velocity control with unity feedback

labs.title: Lab 4: Tracking Control (Trapezoidal Velocity Profile)
objectives: Implement a trapezoidal velocity profile generator for tracking control
Use the PI controller from Lab 3B to track a desired velocity profile
Analyze steady-state tracking error during acceleration, constant, and deceleration phases
Analyze the effect of PI gains on ramp tracking performance
prelab_questions: What is a trapezoidal velocity profile?
What are the three phases of a trapezoidal profile (acceleration, constant, deceleration)?
How do PI gains affect ramp tracking vs step tracking?
Can integral control eliminate steady-state error during a ramp input?
What is a generator function in Python?
important_equations: Acceleration phase: v(t) = v_max * t / t_accel
Constant phase: v(t) = v_max
Deceleration phase: v(t) = v_max * (t_stop - t) / t_decel
Profile length: total number of time steps = (t_accel + t_ss + t_stop) / dt

from profile import trapz_profile_gen, trapz_profile_length
from controller import controller

Kp = 0.5
KI = 1.0
ctrl = controller(Kp, KI)

velocity_log = []
output_log = []

for setpoint in trapz_profile_gen(500, 1, 2, 1, 0.002):
    ctrl.___________(___________)   # (1) update the setpoint each iteration
    current_vel = encoder.get_velocity()
    voltage = ctrl.___________(___________) # (2) run the controller
    motor.set_effort(voltage)
    velocity_log.append(___________) # (3) log current velocity
    output_log.append(___________)   # (4) log controller output

hardware_procedures: Upload profile.py and updated main.py to STM32 via Thonny
Run tracking control experiment with trapezoidal setpoint profile
Collect and plot overlaid ideal vs actual velocity in Jupyter Notebook
Test gain combinations: (Kp=0.19, KI=0.4), (Kp=0.5, KI=1.0), (Kp=0.1, KI=1)
controller_tuning_methods: Optimal (Figure 4): Kp=0.5, KI=1.0 — best tracking, steady-state error eliminated at constant velocity
Steady-state error NOT eliminated during acceleration and deceleration phases
Increasing KI eliminates steady-state error at the constant speed segment
Decreasing Kp causes lagging response during ramp segments
common_mistakes: Expecting integral to eliminate error during ramp — it only eliminates at constant velocity
Using a regular function instead of a generator (yield) for trapz_profile_gen
Off-by-one errors in trapz_profile_length calculation
Using KI in profile.py and Ki elsewhere — report uses 'KI' (uppercase I) in figure captions

labs.title: Lab 5A: Sine Sweep Test (Frequency Response Identification)
objectives: Experimentally determine the frequency response of the pendulum system using a sine sweep input
Generate experimental Bode plots (magnitude and phase) from measured data
Develop and fit a second-order transfer function model to the experimental Bode plot
Understand how wn, zeta, and Kss affect the shape of Bode plots
prelab_questions: What is a sine sweep (chirp) signal and why is it useful for system identification?
What is a Bode plot and what do magnitude and phase plots represent?
What causes a resonant peak in a magnitude Bode plot?
How does damping ratio affect the resonant peak?
What frequency range is reliable for this pendulum experiment?
Why does the data look chaotic after 10 Hz?
important_equations: Second-order TF: G(s) = Kss*wn^2 / (s^2 + 2*zeta*wn*s + wn^2)
Identified parameters: wn = 6.7 rad/s, zeta = 0.41, Kss = 0.018
Magnitude (dB) = 20*log10(|G(jw)|)
Reliable frequency range for pendulum: 2-6 Hz

code_completion_exercises.instructions: Complete the MATLAB snippet that appeared verbatim in the Lab 5A report (Figure 3) to generate the theoretical Bode plot.
code_completion_exercises.blanked_code:
wn = _________;     % (1) identified natural frequency (rad/s)
zeta = _________;   % (2) identified damping ratio
Kss = _________;    % (3) identified steady-state gain

num = Kss*wn^2;
den = [1, _________, _________];  % (4) standard 2nd-order denominator coefficients

G = tf(num, den);
_________           % (5) generate Bode plot
grid on
code_completion_exercises.note: The variable names wn, zeta, Kss, num, den, and G are taken verbatim from the report. The assignment 'den = [1, 2*zeta*wn, wn^2]' was not in the extracted text fragment but is required to complete the tf() call.

hardware_procedures: Connect STM32 to DC motor (pendulum setup) and rotary encoder
Apply linear sine sweep input signal to pendulum system
Reliable sweep range: 2-6 Hz for the long pendulum
Record angular response via encoder in Jupyter Notebook
Transfer data to PC via serial for analysis
controller_tuning_methods: Tune wn to shift resonant peak left/right on frequency axis
Tune zeta to adjust sharpness of resonant peak (lower = sharper)
Tune Kss to shift entire magnitude plot up or down
Match theoretical to experimental only in the reliable 2-6 Hz range
common_mistakes: Trusting Bode data above 10 Hz — sensor noise and mechanical limits make it unreliable
Confusing wn (rad/s) with Hz
Forgetting den = [1, 2*zeta*wn, wn^2] — just writing num without den
Omitting 'grid on' after bode(G)
Trying to match the full frequency range instead of only the reliable 2-6 Hz window
likely_exam_concepts: Write the MATLAB code verbatim from the report to generate the second-order TF Bode plot
Explain how wn, zeta, and Kss each affect the Bode magnitude and phase plots
Identify resonant frequency from a Bode plot
Why is data above 10 Hz unreliable?
What is the reliable frequency range for the pendulum experiment and why?

labs.title: Lab 5B: Closed-Loop Position Control (PID on Pendulum)
objectives: Implement and tune a PID controller for position control of the pendulum
Optimize PID performance by sequentially tuning Kp, then KD, then KI
Understand derivative controller drawbacks (noise amplification from encoder)
Use MATLAB rltool with root locus to validate and analyze the PID design
Learn the effect of sampling rate on derivative control noise
prelab_questions: What does each gain (Kp, KI, KD) do to system performance?
Why does derivative control amplify noise?
How do you use MATLAB rltool for root locus analysis?
What are z1 and z2 zeros in a PID controller?
What is the sequential tuning order for a PID controller?
important_equations: PID control law: u(t) = Kp*e + KI*integral(e)dt + KD*de/dt
PID TF: C(s) = Kp + KI/s + KD*s = (KD*s^2 + Kp*s + KI) / s
PID zeros z1, z2: roots of KD*s^2 + Kp*s + KI = 0

identified_gains_verbatim.source: Report body text, Results section: 'Step response and controller output using optimal PID gains, where Kp = 100, KI = 350, and KD = 5.6'

labs.gain_name_note: The report uses 'KI' and 'KD' (uppercase I and D) in all figure captions and results text. Use this capitalization exactly.
labs.appendix_code_note: The Jupyter Notebook with PID implementation, step response plots, and controller output plots (Appendix A) was submitted as an image and is not extractable as text. The MATLAB rltool workflow is described in the report body text.
reported_matlab_workflow_verbatim: We calculated z1 and z2 in Matlab. This calculation is shown below in Figure 4.
We graphed the root locus and response in Matlab using rltool with added z1 and z2 zeros, and pole at origin.
Root Locus With Optimized Gain Values Kp=100, Ki=350, Kd=5.6
Time Step Response for Gain Values of Kp=100, Ki=350, Kd=5.6

reported_effects_verbatim.P_control: The proportional controller increased the speed of the response and reduced the overall error by applying a correction proportional to the position error. As the proportional gain increased, the system responded faster, but excessive proportional gain caused larger oscillations and overshoot.
reported_effects_verbatim.D_control: The derivative controller improved the damping of the system by responding to how quickly the error changed over time. This reduced overshoot and helped stabilize the pendulum more quickly. However, high derivative gains amplified measurement noise from the encoder, which introduced vibrations into the controller output.
reported_effects_verbatim.I_control: The integral controller minimized the steady-state error by accumulating the error over time and continuously correcting small remaining offsets from the setpoint. While this improved long-term accuracy, excessive integral gain increased oscillation and overshoot because the stored accumulated error continued driving the system even after the pendulum approached the desired position.
reported_effects_verbatim.sampling_rate: Increasing the sampling period reduced the effective sampling frequency of the controller. This helped reduce vibrations because the derivative controller became less sensitive to high-frequency [noise], producing a smoother controller output and a more stable response.

matlab_simulink_exercises: Calculate z1 and z2 zeros from PID numerator polynomial: roots([KD, Kp, KI])
Use rltool to plot root locus with PID zeros z1, z2 and pole at origin
Graph closed-loop step response using rltool
Verify optimized gains (Kp=100, KI=350, KD=5.6) give stable response

code_completion_exercises.instructions: Complete the MATLAB code to compute z1 and z2 from the PID gains used in Lab 5B, as described in the report.
code_completion_exercises.note: The report uses capitalization KI and KD (not Ki and Kd) in all results text and figure captions.

blanks.prompt: The proportional controller increased __________ and reduced __________.
blanks.answer: the speed of the response / the overall error

blanks.prompt: High derivative gains amplified __________ from the encoder, which introduced __________ into the controller output.
The integral controller minimized __________ by accumulating the error over time.
Increasing the sampling period reduced the effective __________ of the controller.

hardware_procedures: Connect STM32 to pendulum motor and rotary encoder
Upload PID main.py to STM32 via Thonny
Tuning order: Kp first, then KD, then KI
Reduce sampling rate if KD causes noise-induced vibration
Verify controller output stays within saturation limits
controller_tuning_methods: Sequential order from report: Kp -> KD -> KI
Optimal: Kp=100, KI=350, KD=5.6
High KD amplifies encoder noise -> reduce sampling period to mitigate
Excessive KI: accumulated error causes overshoot past setpoint
common_mistakes: Using lowercase ki and kd instead of KI and KD — the report uses uppercase
Tuning KI before KD
Not reducing sampling period when high KD causes vibration
Integral windup: large KI causes overshoot because accumulated error keeps driving system
Passing wrong coefficient order to roots() — must be [KD, Kp, KI] for z1 and z2
likely_exam_concepts: Write MATLAB code to compute z1 and z2 zeros using roots([KD, Kp, KI])
Explain effects of P, D, I gains on position control response using language from the report
Describe the sequential PID tuning procedure (Kp -> KD -> KI)
Why does derivative control amplify noise and how is it mitigated?
Use rltool with z1, z2 zeros and pole at origin to analyze the PID design

DC_motor_model.units: Kss in rad/s/V, tau in seconds

optimal_PID_position_gains.capitalization_note: Report uses KI and KD (uppercase I and D) throughout

system_identification_methods.first_order_from_step: Apply step input, measure steady-state velocity and time to 63.2% -> K and tau
system_identification_methods.second_order_from_sine_sweep: Apply sine sweep, generate experimental Bode plot, tune wn/zeta/Kss until theoretical matches

controller_types_summary.P: Fast response; residual steady-state error for type-0 plant
controller_types_summary.I: Eliminates steady-state error; slows transient; can cause oscillation
controller_types_summary.D: Improves damping; reduces overshoot; amplifies encoder noise
controller_types_summary.PI: Combines fast response with steady-state error elimination
controller_types_summary.PD: Fast response with improved damping; still has steady-state error
controller_types_summary.PID: Full control: speed + damping + error elimination

capitalization_traps.Lab 3B Ki: lowercase i — 'Ki = 0.5' in report body text
capitalization_traps.Lab 5B KI and KD: uppercase I and D — 'KI= 350, and KD = 5.6' in report body text
